case_name: Removal of Vorlan from well water for drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for Vinclozolin-contaminated well water intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A rural drinking water utility draws from a shallow production well influenced by nearby agricultural land and has detected the fungicide Vorlan in the raw water. The utility needs a clear, reliable treatment approach that can be implemented with established drinking water technologies rather than highly specialized systems. The water is otherwise a typical low-turbidity groundwater, and the utility prefers robust operation with moderate cost and manageable maintenance.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve strong reduction of Vinclozolin to a level suitable for drinking water protection using established treatment practice and maintain stable potable water quality.
secondary_goals: use proven drinking water treatment technology
maintain reliable performance under routine utility operation

core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb hydrophobic synthetic organic micropollutants such as Vinclozolin from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and provide effluent polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in the finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: Vinclozolin is an organic pesticide-type contaminant for which activated carbon is a conventional and well-supported control technology in drinking water practice
the source is low-turbidity well water, which is favorable for direct adsorption treatment without extensive pretreatment
the utility prefers proven technology, and GAC has broad public guidance and field experience for pesticide and taste-and-odor precursor removal
energy demand is low and operation is manageable for a small municipal system
potential_risks: media exhaustion and breakthrough require routine monitoring and timely carbon replacement or reactivation
natural organic matter can reduce adsorption bed life
if disinfection is chlorination, carbon performance and biological growth control in the bed need operational attention

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_dosing: adsorb dissolved Vinclozolin before solids separation
core_unit_functions.coagulation: capture PAC and associated contaminants in formed flocs
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove flocculated solids and PAC particles

applicability_rationale: activated carbon is again the key proven mechanism for pesticide control
PAC can be a flexible option when contamination is intermittent or seasonal due to agricultural influence
the train uses established drinking water unit processes rather than specialized advanced systems
it can be implemented where the utility already has or can support chemical feed and filtration
potential_risks: performance is dose-dependent and requires jar testing or site optimization
continuous solids handling and sludge production are higher than for fixed-bed GAC
less attractive than GAC for a stable groundwater application with a single dissolved organic contaminant

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide physical rejection of dissolved organic micropollutants including pesticide residues
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust mineral balance and corrosivity after membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in distributed water

applicability_rationale: pressure membranes are technically capable of reducing many low-level organic contaminants including pesticide compounds
this route can be effective if future broader contaminant control is needed in addition to Vinclozolin
the low-turbidity well water is favorable for membrane pretreatment requirements
potential_risks: higher capital and energy use than needed for a single-contaminant easy case
concentrate management creates an additional residuals burden
post-treatment stabilization is necessary to produce finished drinking water of acceptable chemistry

core_unit_functions.biologically_active_or_gac_filtration: remove oxidation byproducts and provide polishing

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can degrade certain organic micropollutants when adsorption alone is insufficient or when multiple trace organics are present
a downstream filtration or carbon step is commonly needed to polish transformation products
the approach is technically feasible but less conventional for a small groundwater system focused on one pesticide
potential_risks: process control is more demanding than adsorption-based treatment
incomplete oxidation can form transformation products that still require polishing and verification
chemical and energy demands are higher than for GAC

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: attempt transfer of volatile compounds from water to air

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranking comparator because it is a recognized treatment unit for some organic compounds
however it is not a preferred mainstream approach for a fungicide like Vinclozolin in groundwater
potential_risks: Vinclozolin is not sufficiently suited to air stripping, so removal would likely be unreliable
off-gas handling could be needed if volatilization were relevant
selection of this option would not align well with proven treatment practice for pesticide removal
